bodrum.metaTitle: Bodrum & Gulf of Gökova Itineraries | Boat4You
bodrum.metaDesc: Routes from Bodrum: the Gökova gulf's pine bays, Cleopatra's island and long lazy swims — Turkish coast sailing, day by day.
bodrum.title: The gulet coast, sailed your way
bodrum.description: Bodrum faces a gulf that was made for wandering. Gökova cuts thirty miles into the Turkish mainland, its shores folded into bay after pine-backed bay where the routine writes itself: swim, breakfast, two hours of sailing, another bay. Sedir island keeps its ancient theatre and a beach of sand the legends say Cleopatra shipped in; English Harbour hides so well the Royal Navy used it in wartime. Restaurants with their own jetties will take your lines, feed you meze and charge you fairly for the pleasure. The meltemi reaches in here softened, more breeze than weather. Bodrum itself — castle, market, marina — bookends the week properly. It's the easiest introduction to Turkey a sailor can get.

gocek.metaDesc: Routes from Göcek and Fethiye through Skopea Limani's twelve islands: jetty restaurants, ruins above the anchorages, easy miles.
gocek.title: Twelve islands inside one sheltered gulf
gocek.description: Göcek sits at the head of Skopea Limani, a gulf so sheltered and so full of islands that you can spend a week without ever taking a wave over the bow. The formula is unbeatable for mixed crews: anchor drops in ten metres of clear green water, a lazy line goes ashore to a pine, and dinner happens at a family restaurant that built its jetty exactly for you. Lycian ruins stand right above several anchorages — Cleopatra's Baths, the tombs at Kaya — and Fethiye's market supplies the galley properly. Winds are light and thermal, navigation is line-of-sight, marinas in Göcek are plentiful and slick. If Turkey has a nursery slope for chartering, this is it — and it happens to be beautiful.

bodrum-akyaka.metaTitle: 7-Day Gökova Gulf Loop from Bodrum | Boat4You
bodrum-akyaka.metaDesc: Sail seven days round the Gulf of Gökova from Bodrum: Mersincik's sea cave, Akyaka's Azmak River, Ören's Keramos ruins and the Çökertme horseshoe bay.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 15-mile first leg south from Bodrum to Mersincik, a granite-mouthed bay with a kayakable sea cave and no crowds.
1.description: Clear Bodrum Marina after the handover paperwork and point the bow south-southeast. Fifteen miles of open water lets you find the boat's feet before anything technical, and by early afternoon the walls of Mersincik come up on the port bow, guarding a bay you enter through a gap in the granite. Drop the hook on sand in four to six metres; holding is good and the swell rarely follows you in. There's nothing ashore, which is the point. At the head of the bay a low sea cave sits waiting for a kayak, and the rocky edges hide grottoes worth a mask and fins. We usually climb the thyme-covered ridge for the light before dinner. Stock the fridge in Bodrum first, because the nearest shop is a long way back.
thingsToDo: Paddle a kayak into the Mersincik sea cave
Snorkel the granite grottoes near the entrance
Hike the wild-thyme ridge above the bay
Sundowners on deck in a boat-free anchorage
1.mooringTip: Anchor free on sand in 4–6 m near the head; holding is reliable and the bay stays flat in the usual northwesterly. No quay, water or fuel here, so top up tanks and provisions in Bodrum before you leave.

2.shortDescription: A short eight-mile hop east to Kairos, a hushed cove where a single quay restaurant lends its buoys and Lycian stones watch from above.
2.description: No need to rush this one. Eight miles of flat morning water bring you to Kairos, a fold in the southern shore that most crews have never heard of. A family runs the only restaurant on the water, and their buoys are yours for the price of dinner, so pick up a line and settle in. The pebble beach shelves quickly into clear water; behind it, a rough path climbs to the tumbled stones of a Lycian lookout, best walked in the cool of the evening. Lunch is easy here. Order a balık ekmek at the quay grill, eat it with your feet in the sea, and let the afternoon go slow. Nights are properly quiet, with little more than the odd gull for company and the boat swinging gently on the buoy.
thingsToDo: Pick up a restaurant buoy at Kairos
Climb to the Lycian lookout ruins
Balık ekmek at the quay grill
Snorkel straight off the pebble beach
2.mooringTip: Take one of the restaurant's buoys and pay by eating ashore; the cove is snug and holds you off the north wind. If the buoys are full, anchor on sand and pebble in 4–6 m and back toward the beach.

3.shortDescription: Twelve miles east lands you at Karaca, a fishing inlet backed by pine and oleander where the day's catch turns up grilled on the quay.
3.description: Leave Kairos after breakfast for the twelve-mile run to Karaca, a working inlet where fishing boats still outnumber charter yachts. The approach is green: pine comes right down to the water and oleander lines the pebble strand. As at the coves before it, the local restaurant owns the buoys, and dinner secures your spot. Take the cliff path that threads through pine and thyme above the bay; the smell alone is worth the climb, and the view back over the anchored fleet is a good one. Slip into the water along the rocks and you'll find damselfish hanging in the shallows. When the fishermen come in, a glass of çay on the quay buys you an hour of talk. Ask for the red mullet grilled simply with lemon and oil, and you won't want much else.
thingsToDo: Walk the pine-and-thyme cliff path
Snorkel for damselfish along the rocks
Share çay with the Karaca fishermen
Grilled red mullet at the quay taverna
3.mooringTip: Karaca works the same way as its neighbours: pick up the restaurant buoy, settle the bill over dinner. Anchoring room is tight, so if you drop the hook use sand in 4–6 m and keep clear of the buoyed swing circles.

4.shortDescription: Eight miles to the head of the gulf and Akyaka, a wood-balconied town on the Azmak where kitesurfers ride the afternoon wind.
4.description: This is the head of the gulf, and the wind knows it. The Gökova funnel puts a real breeze through Akyaka most summer afternoons, which is why the kite and windsurf crowd base themselves here, so make the short eight-mile passage in the calm of the morning and be tied up before it fills in. The town rewards the effort. Ottoman-style houses with carved wooden balconies climb the slope, and the Azmak River runs cold and glass-clear out of the hills behind. Take the kayak a couple of kilometres up it, past herons in the reeds and, if you're lucky and quiet, an otter. Back in town, find a lokanta on the riverbank and order manti, the little dumplings drowned in garlic yoghurt. It's a livelier stop than the coves, in the best way.
thingsToDo: Kayak two kilometres up the Azmak River
Wander the Ottoman wood-balcony old town
Manti at a riverbank lokanta
Watch the kitesurfers off the beach
4.mooringTip: Akyaka's small marina takes you stern-to at roughly €60–90 a night, worth booking ahead in July and August. Arrive by mid-morning: the thermal wind builds fast here and berthing gets awkward once it's blowing hard. Anchoring in the bay is possible on sand in 4–6 m but exposed to the afternoon breeze.

5.shortDescription: Twelve miles back along the southern shore to Ören, an olive-farming village where the ancient stones of Keramos lie scattered among the wildflowers.
5.description: Slip your lines while it's still calm and run twelve miles down the southern shore to Ören. Olive terraces rise behind the beach, and the pace drops back to village time after the buzz of Akyaka. The reason to stop is Keramos, a fourth-century-BC city whose walls and column drums lie half-lost in the grass and wildflowers just inland; go in the morning before the heat and you'll likely have the ruins to yourself. If your timing lands on market day, the square fills with sacks of spice and stacks of local ceramics worth a rummage. Lunch is a beach affair here, a plate off the mangal and a cold ayran under a straw umbrella. Come evening, the smell of cumin-rubbed lamb over coals drifts along the sand, and it's hard to resist.
thingsToDo: Explore the 4th-century-BC Keramos ruins
Browse the weekly Ören market stalls
Beach lunch with a cold ayran
Cumin-lamb mangal barbecue on the sand
5.mooringTip: Ören's little marina is the cheapest berth of the trip, stern-to at around €40–60 a night. It fills less than Akyaka but a call ahead in peak weeks does no harm. Anchoring off the beach on sand in 4–6 m works well in the prevailing northwesterly.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen miles west to Çökertme, a pine-ringed horseshoe famous for its folk song, family tavernas, and a late-summer glow in the water.
6.description: A longer leg today, fifteen miles west across the mouth of the gulf to Çökertme, a horseshoe bay wrapped in pine. Turkish sailors know it from an old folk song, and on summer nights someone at the quay usually picks up a saz and plays it. The bay works on the familiar buoy-for-dinner arrangement, with a handful of family tavernas competing gently for your trade. Order köfte grilled over olivewood and you'll understand the fuss. Late in August, if the algae are blooming, a swim off the stern after dark lights the water blue-green around your arms, one of those things nobody quite believes until they see it. There's a ridge walk through the pines above the anchorage for the restless. Otherwise, this is a bay that asks you to stay put and do very little.
thingsToDo: Night swim in the bioluminescent water (late Aug)
Köfte grilled over olivewood at a taverna
Live saz folk music on the quay
Walk the pine ridge above the bay
6.mooringTip: Same deal as the coves: take a taverna's buoy and pay with dinner, which the restaurants here take seriously. Anchoring is fine too, on sand in 5–7 m off the beach, and the horseshoe holds you snug from the north. Weekends in August get busy, so arrive by late afternoon for a good spot.

7.shortDescription: A final fifteen miles back to Bodrum, with time for one last swim before the marina, the castle, and a farewell drink over the harbour.
7.description: The last leg is an easy fifteen miles back to Bodrum, and there's no reason to hurry it. Anchor off Çökertme one more time for a morning swim if the crew is willing, then lay a course west for the marina. Call ahead the day before to lock in your handover slot, and swing by the fuel berth at the harbour entrance before you take your berth on lazy lines. With the boat squared away, the afternoon is yours ashore. The Castle of St Peter guards the harbour from a fifteenth-century footing and now holds the underwater-archaeology museum, well worth an hour. After that, lose yourself in the bazaar and haggle for leather and saffron. End the trip the right way, with a drink at a harbourside meyhane as the sun drops behind the masts.
thingsToDo: Tour the 15th-century Castle of St Peter
Haggle for leather and saffron in the bazaar
Final swim off Çökertme at dawn
Sunset drink at a harbourside meyhane
7.mooringTip: Bodrum Marina berths you stern-to on lazy lines, roughly €80–130 a night in high season. Confirm your handover time with the base 24 hours out, and stop at the fuel berth by the entrance to fill up before you drop into your slot.

bodrum-didim.metaDesc: A 7-day yacht charter from Bodrum up the Turkish Aegean: Yalıkavak, Güllük, Didim's Apollo temple, Kuşadası for Ephesus, then a downwind run home.

1.shortDescription: Ease into the charter at Bodrum Marina: stock the boat, climb the Castle of St Peter, and stand among the Mausoleum's ruins.
1.description: Most crews spend the first day getting the boat ready rather than rushing off, and Bodrum rewards the pause. The marina sits below the Castle of St Peter, a fifteenth-century Crusader fortress that now holds the Museum of Underwater Archaeology — the Uluburun wreck inside is one of the oldest ever excavated. Give it a couple of hours. A short walk inland brings you to the scattered stones of the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus, once counted among the Seven Wonders. Not much stands, but the scale still registers. Load fresh produce, water and ice before the afternoon meltemi picks up, then find a waterfront meyhane at dusk for meze and a glass of rakı. Refuel at the fuel berth by the entrance so you leave with full tanks tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Tour the Museum of Underwater Archaeology
Find the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus stones
Haggle in the Bodrum bazaar for spices
Meze and rakı at a waterfront meyhane
1.mooringTip: Bodrum Marina berths stern-to with lazy lines and shelters you fully; reckon on €80–130 a night at peak. Top up at the fuel berth by the harbour entrance before you head north.

2.shortDescription: Round the peninsula's tip to glossy Yalıkavak, or duck into calmer Göltürkbükü where wooden platforms hang over the water.
2.description: Twelve miles takes you around the northwest corner of the peninsula, so expect the meltemi on the nose for the last stretch — worth leaving by nine to beat the afternoon build. Yalıkavak's Palmarina is the show-off stop: superyachts, designer alleys and a marble quay that opened in 2014. It is an easy place to lose an afternoon window-shopping, less easy on the wallet. Four miles east, Göltürkbükü is the gentler choice. The bay is ringed with pine and the beach clubs run their swimming platforms out over shallow water. Anchor off, swim before lunch, then take the tender in for a long, lazy meal. Gümüşlük and its sunken Myndos ruins lie just west if you fancy a seafood dinner and a sunset ashore.
thingsToDo: Walk the Palmarina superyacht quay
Swim off Göltürkbükü's platform beach clubs
Sunset seafood at Gümüşlük's sunken Myndos
Browse the boutiques along Palmarina's alleys
2.mooringTip: Palmarina takes you stern-to but charges premium rates, often €200–400 a night in July. Göltürkbükü's small harbour runs €100–160, or simply anchor off the beach clubs on sand in 4–6 m.

3.shortDescription: Cross into the Gulf of Mandalya to Güllük, a working fishing town with the Carian ruins of Iasos on the headland.
3.description: South and west across the Gulf of Mandalya, Güllük feels a world away from the marina glamour behind you. This is a real fishing port: boats land their catch at the quay, and the fish you eat at dinner probably came off one that morning. The pace slows the moment you tie up. The reason to stop, beyond the quiet, is Iasos. The ancient Carian-Greek city sits on the headland a short walk or tender-ride south, with a Roman agora, Byzantine walls and a theatre looking back over the water. Go in the cooler evening light. Afterwards, the quayside grills do çöp şiş, the thin lamb skewers this corner of the Aegean is known for, and the bay itself stays glassy calm once the meltemi drops.
thingsToDo: Walk the Carian and Roman ruins of Iasos
Eat çöp şiş at the fishing quay
Swim in glassy Güllük Bay
Watch the day's catch land at dawn
3.mooringTip: Güllük's small marina takes you stern-to at €60–100 a night and shelters well from the north. If it is full, anchor on good holding sand in 5–7 m inside the bay.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles north to Didim, where the colossal Temple of Apollo still stands and Altınkum's golden sand waits a mile south.
4.description: A twenty-mile leg brings you to Didim and one of the great sights of the Turkish coast. The Temple of Apollo at Didyma was among the largest ever raised by the Greeks, and even half-ruined its columns run nineteen metres into the sky. This was the oracle of the Branchidae, second only to Delphi in its day. It sits a couple of kilometres inland, an easy taxi from the marina. D-Marin Didim is a big, well-run harbour and the standard overnight here, sheltered whatever the wind does. Once you have seen the temple, drop down to Altınkum, a mile south, for soft golden sand and shallow swimming. Pick up a box of lokum from a family confectioner in town before you leave.
thingsToDo: Stand beneath the Temple of Apollo's columns
Swim off Altınkum's golden sand
Buy lokum from a family confectioner
Browse the harbour bazaar for textiles
4.mooringTip: D-Marin Didim berths stern-to with lazy lines, fully sheltered, around €100–160 a night at peak. It is popular in season, so call ahead to hold a slot and refuel at the entrance berth.

5.shortDescription: A long 32-mile run north to Kuşadası, your springboard for Ephesus, the greatest Roman city on this coast.
5.description: This is the longest passage of the outbound half, thirty-two miles into the prevailing north wind, so treat it as a proper sailing day and start early. The reward at the far end is Ephesus, and it is worth every tack. Kuşadası is a busy cruise port with a full-service marina, so book the berth. From there Ephesus is a short taxi and a half-day on foot: the Library of Celsus, the Great Theatre that once held twenty-five thousand, marble streets you can still walk. Go first thing before the tour buses and the heat arrive. Back in town, Ladies Beach (Kadınlar Denizi) is where locals swim, and Güvercinada's little castle guards the harbour. The bazaar is good for silk and spices if you have time.
thingsToDo: Explore Ephesus and the Library of Celsus
Sit in the Great Theatre's stone tiers
Swim at Ladies Beach (Kadınlar Denizi)
Climb Güvercinada's harbour castle
5.mooringTip: Setur Kuşadası Marina berths stern-to, fully sheltered, and runs roughly €100–160 a night at peak. It is busy in cruise season, so reserve ahead and refuel at the entrance berth before the long haul back.

6.shortDescription: A gentle fifteen miles to quiet Özdere, with the oracle sanctuary of Claros inland and empty coves for the paddleboard.
6.description: After the crowds of Kuşadası, Özdere is a deliberate change of gear. Fifteen easy miles up the coast bring you to a low-key resort strip that most charter routes skip, which is exactly its appeal. The beaches run long and shallow, the coves between them stay quiet, and a paddleboard is the best way to nose along the shore. Inland, above Ahmetbeyli, lie the ruins of Claros, an oracle sanctuary of Apollo with a temple and the stumps of colossal statues. It sees a fraction of Ephesus's visitors. Back aboard, this stretch is olive country, so look for zeytinyağlı vegetable dishes cooked in local oil and a cool Aegean white to go with a taverna dinner ashore. A calm, restful night before the long haul south.
thingsToDo: Visit the oracle ruins of Claros
Paddleboard the quiet coves along the shore
Try zeytinyağlı olive-oil dishes at a taverna
Sample a chilled Aegean white wine
6.mooringTip: Özdere's small harbour is modest and cheap at around €40–70 a night, sheltered from the north. Infrastructure is limited, so if you would rather swing free, anchor on sand in 4–6 m off the bay.

7.shortDescription: Sixty downwind miles home to Bodrum, with an optional soak in Kara Ada's hot springs before the final tie-up.
7.description: The last day is the big one: sixty miles back to Bodrum. The saving grace is direction. Everything you clawed into on the way up now sits behind you, so this is a downwind run with the meltemi doing the work — fast, but it can get lumpy, so reef early and leave at first light. Aim to be off the peninsula by mid-afternoon. If there is time and the sea allows, Kara Ada, the island off Bodrum, has warm sulphur springs where you can anchor and swim in bath-temperature water. It is a fitting last stop. Refuel before you berth, give the boat a rinse, and confirm your handover slot for the morning. Then it is one more sunset over the castle and a final crew dinner ashore to close the week.
thingsToDo: Soak in Kara Ada's warm sulphur springs
Rinse and refuel back at Bodrum Marina
Final crew dinner above the harbour
Last sunset over the Castle of St Peter
7.mooringTip: Back in Bodrum Marina it is stern-to with lazy lines, €80–130 a night. Refuel at the entrance berth on the way in and confirm your handover slot 24 hours ahead so the morning stays relaxed.

bodrum.metaTitle: 7-Day Gökova Gulf Loop from Bodrum | Boat4You
bodrum.metaDesc: A week-long yacht charter from Bodrum around the Gulf of Gökova: thermal springs at Kara Ada, Çökertme's buoys, Cleopatra's beach and calm Mersincik.

1.shortDescription: A gentle four-mile hop south to Black Island, where warm mineral springs bubble up beside a sand cove made for the first swim.
1.description: Kara Ada sits just four miles below Bodrum, which makes day one less a passage than a warm-up. We usually slip the marina lines mid-afternoon, once the boat is stowed and the paperwork is done, and let the crew find their sea legs on flat water. The island is volcanic, and its south end has shallow pools fed by warm mineral springs; locals have soaked here for generations, and the short dinghy trip ashore is worth it. Drop the hook in the cove on good holding sand, four to six metres, tucked in from the north. That matters in July and August, when the Meltemi funnels down from the north at fifteen to twenty-five knots and strengthens through the afternoon. There is no quay and no shop, so top up water and provisions before you leave.
thingsToDo: Bathe in the warm spring pools ashore
Snorkel the black volcanic ledges
Sundowners and meze in the cove
Scramble the goat tracks for the view
1.mooringTip: Anchoring is free in the cove; the sand holds well at four to six metres and screens you from the northerly. Nothing ashore to tie to, so if you want power and water, Bodrum Marina is four miles back and runs €80–130 a night in peak season.

2.shortDescription: Fifteen miles east into calmer gulf water to Çökertme, a pine-ringed bay where the restaurant buoys come free if you dine ashore.
2.description: Turn east from Kara Ada and the character of the sailing changes. Once you round into the Gulf of Gökova the swell settles and the wind eases off the outer coast, so the fifteen miles to Çökertme tend to pass easily under a broad reach. The bay opens as a wide horseshoe with pine coming down to the shore, quiet enough that you hear the cicadas before you spot the moorings. A handful of family restaurants lay out buoys, and the deal is old and simple: take a buoy, have dinner under the vines, and you pay nothing for the night. That name belongs to an old boatmen's song still sung along this coast. If you would rather lie to your own anchor, the sand holds at five to seven metres with good shelter from the north. Grilled köfte and a cold Efes usually close the evening.
thingsToDo: Köfte grilled over olive wood ashore
Night swim in the still bay
Walk up through the pines above
Listen for the old Çökertme song
2.mooringTip: Grab one of the restaurant buoys and your only bill is dinner. If they are full, or you prefer privacy, anchor mid-bay on sand at five to seven metres, well protected from the north. No need to book ahead outside the August peak.

3.shortDescription: Deeper into the gulf to Sedir Island, home to a fabled golden beach you may swim to but never walk across.
3.description: Another fifteen miles carries you east to Sedir Adası, better known abroad as Cleopatra Island. The story goes that Mark Antony shipped the fine golden sand here from North Africa for the queen, and grain analysis does place its origin well south of the Aegean, so the legend has more legs than most. The catch: the beach is protected, and rangers allow swimming from the water but no footsteps on the sand itself. Come by tender and float, do not stroll. Above the shore lie the walls and small theatre of ancient Kedreai, an easy wander among pines and fallen columns. Snorkellers can trace the old harbour moles just under the surface. Anchor in the channel on the north side, sand at five to seven metres, sheltered from the prevailing northerly. Arrive by early afternoon; the day boats from Marmaris thin out later.
thingsToDo: Swim to the roped-off Cleopatra Beach
Wander the ruins of ancient Kedreai
Snorkel the sunken harbour walls
Sea bass and rakı at a quay table
3.mooringTip: Anchor off the north channel on sand at five to seven metres, shielded from the north. This is a national-park zone, so respect the rope lines on the beach and keep off the sand. Get in before the afternoon tour boats crowd the anchorage.

4.shortDescription: A short dozen miles across to the gulf's quiet southern shore and Karaca, a fishing hamlet fringed with oleander and pebble.
4.description: Karaca lies twelve miles across the gulf on its southern shore, a fold in the coast where a small fishing village tucks behind a pebble beach. Oleander crowds the water's edge and the pace drops another notch; this is the sort of place where the afternoon is spent doing very little. The restaurants here run the same buoy-for-dinner deal as Çökertme, so a table ashore earns you a free night on the mooring. We tend to arrive by mid-afternoon, swim off the stern, then row in for an early rakı. The holding in the inlet is good sand at four to six metres, and the hills close it off from the northerly completely. Ask about the lamb turned on a spit; it is the local specialty and worth the wait. Bring cash, as card machines are unreliable this far along.
thingsToDo: Hunt for sea glass along the shingle
Backgammon under the old fig tree
Spit-roast lamb at a family table
Cool off the stern in glassy water
4.mooringTip: Take a restaurant buoy and settle the mooring with the price of dinner. Prefer independence? The inlet holds well on sand at four to six metres, fully boxed in from the north. It rarely fills, so no booking needed.

5.shortDescription: Backtrack west to Mersincik, a granite-guarded bay with a sea cave at its head, thyme on the hills and no lights for miles.
5.description: Turn the bow west and retrace fifteen miles along the gulf to Mersincik, one of the wilder anchorages on this loop. The entrance narrows between grey granite, and at the head of the bay a low sea cave invites a paddle in with the kayak or tender. There is nothing built here, which is rather the point. Behind the beach a rough path climbs a ridge thick with wild thyme; crush a sprig between your fingers and you will smell why the honey along this coast is prized. Holding is reliable sand at four to six metres, and the surrounding hills shut out swell from every quarter. Because it sits inside the protected Gökova reserve, there is no shop and no fuel, so stock the fridge and fill the tanks before you leave Karaca. After dark the sky opens up; with no town for miles, the stars come down to the water.
thingsToDo: Paddle the kayak into the sea cave
Climb the thyme ridge above the bay
Cold zeytinyağlı dishes served on deck
Stargaze with zero light pollution
5.mooringTip: Free anchoring throughout on sand at four to six metres, with all-round shelter from the granite hills. There is no marina and no provisioning, so arrive fully stocked and with full water and fuel.

6.shortDescription: The last leg back to Bodrum, where a Crusader castle guards the harbour and holds one of the oldest shipwrecks ever raised.
6.description: The final passage runs fifteen miles back to Bodrum, ideally sailed in the morning before the Meltemi builds. As the town rises ahead, the honey-coloured Castle of St Peter dominates the harbour mouth, thrown up by the Knights of St John in the fifteenth century. Inside it now sits the Museum of Underwater Archaeology, and its centrepiece is the Uluburun wreck, a Bronze Age trader that went down more than three thousand years ago with a cargo of copper, glass and ivory. Give yourself an afternoon for it. Afterwards the back streets and bazaar reward a slow browse, especially for hand-loomed cotton and the local peştemal towels; haggling is expected and half the fun. Call the marina a day ahead to confirm your slot, and swing by the fuel berth to top up before you take a stern-to berth for the night.
thingsToDo: Tour the Castle of St Peter museum
Stand before the Uluburun shipwreck finds
Haggle for peştemal towels in the bazaar
Top up fuel at the harbour berth
6.mooringTip: Bodrum Marina berths stern-to on lazy lines, roughly €80–130 a night at peak. Fuel up at the berth by the entrance before you go in, and phone ahead to lock your slot, since space is tight in high summer.

7.shortDescription: A calm final morning at the dock: hand the boat back, one last swim and a Turkish coffee before the short run to the airport.
7.description: Checkout comes early. Most bases want the boat alongside and handed over by nine, so it pays to be packed the night before and to run a last look through lockers and bilges at first light. The base manager walks the deck with you, checks the inventory, and notes anything amiss; photograph any mark that goes on the sheet before you sign, and keep a copy. Deposits usually clear back to your card within a week. If your flight is later in the day, there is no rush to leave the quay: order a Turkish coffee at a harbourside café, or slip out to a nearby beach club for a final swim. When it is time, Bodrum airport is a straightforward thirty-five-minute drive, and the marina can arrange a transfer. A relaxed end to a week that stayed sheltered the whole way round.
thingsToDo: Walk the handover with the base manager
Final swim at a Bodrum beach club
Turkish coffee on the harbourside quay
Arrange the transfer to Bodrum airport
7.mooringTip: Have the boat back on its berth before nine for the handover. Photograph anything logged as damage before you sign the inspection sheet. The deposit is normally returned within seven days.

fethiye.metaDesc: Seven-day yacht charter loop from Fethiye: Gemiler, Butterfly Valley, Ölüdeniz Blue Lagoon, Kapı Creek, Ruin Bay and Sarsala, all in the sheltered gulf.

1.shortDescription: An easy eight-mile warm-up south to Gemiler Island, where Byzantine ruins climb the hillside and cold spring water meets the sea across the bay.
1.description: The first leg is deliberately gentle, eight miles down the coast while everyone finds their sea legs and works out where the winch handles live. Gemiler sits just off the mainland, its hillside stacked with the roofless shells of Byzantine churches and cisterns. This was St Nicholas Island long before the saint's relics were carried off to Bari, and the walking paths that thread the ruins are worth an hour before the light goes flat. We usually cross to Coldwater Bay for the night. Freshwater springs feed the shallows here, and you feel it the moment you dive: a cold seam sliding under the warm surface layer. Take a mask and swim the rock edge. Dinner is meze at the quay taverna, and the buoy comes free with the table.
thingsToDo: Walk the Byzantine church ruins on Gemiler
Climb the vaulted path to the summit church
Snorkel the cold spring seam in Coldwater Bay
Meze dinner at the Coldwater quay taverna
1.mooringTip: Coldwater Bay has free restaurant buoys, so take one and settle your bill over dinner. If they are full, the sand holds well at five to seven metres just off the buoy field.

2.shortDescription: A four-mile skip to Butterfly Valley's steep canyon, then round the corner to overnight below Ölüdeniz's famous lagoon and the paragliders off Babadağ.
2.description: Short day, so there is no rush. Butterfly Valley opens as a green cleft in the cliffs, a gorge that climbs more than three hundred metres inland to a waterfall at its head. The Jersey tiger butterflies that give it its name gather here in high summer and nowhere else on the coast. Anchor off, walk in through the reeds, and go for the waterfall if the water is running. It is a daytime stop only; the canyon funnels a gusty afternoon draught that makes for a poor night. We move a mile east to Ölüdeniz for that. The lagoon itself is protected, but the open bay outside holds fine on sand in five to seven metres. Look up around six and you will usually see the paragliders spiralling down off Babadağ.
thingsToDo: Hike the gorge to Butterfly Valley waterfall
Anchor swim off the Ölüdeniz Blue Lagoon
Watch paragliders land off Babadağ mountain
Spot the Jersey tiger butterflies in summer
2.mooringTip: Treat Butterfly Valley as a lunch stop only, dropping on the rocky bottom in six to eight metres and keeping a watch on the swing. For the night, anchor in the open Ölüdeniz bay on sand at five to seven metres, clear of the marked swimming zone.

3.shortDescription: The week's longest leg, twelve miles into the sheltered gulf, ending in Kapı Creek, a narrow inlet with a pebble beach and one taverna.
3.description: This is the biggest passage of the trip, so we like to slip the buoy by nine and get the miles in before the afternoon breeze fills. Rounding into Skopea Limanı, the water flattens and the pine hills close around you. Kapı Creek is tucked at the end of a slot that pinches to about eighty metres at its mouth, which makes it feel more like a Norwegian fjord than a Turkish bay. Inside, it is glassy and fully enclosed. There is a pebble beach at the head for a swim and a little taverna that runs the buoy field. Tie stern-to, run a long line ashore if the boy asks, and settle in. Order the mezes, a jar of thyme honey for the morning, and let the crew paddle out to the narrows before dinner.
thingsToDo: Paddle to the 80-metre creek entrance
Swim off the pebble beach at the head
Tie stern-to at the Kapı Creek taverna
Buy thyme honey and rakı at the quay
3.mooringTip: Kapı Creek is stern-to against the taverna pontoon or on its buoys, both free if you eat ashore, and it fills fast on July evenings. Arrive by early afternoon in peak season to be sure of a spot; the sand at five to seven metres is your fallback.

4.shortDescription: A gentle four miles to Ruin Bay, where a drowned Roman-era ruin lies in two metres of water beneath the Lycian tombs.
4.description: Another lazy morning is fine, because Ruin Bay is only four miles on. The draw here is under the boat rather than ashore. Part of an old Lycian settlement slid into the sea long ago, and its stonework now sits in two or three metres of clear water along the base of the cliff, close enough to snorkel without ever kicking deep. Swim slowly and you will pick out worked blocks and column drums furred with weed. Above the bay, a rough track leads up to the scattered ruins of Lydae, shared these days with goats and old olive trees. It is a good sundowner spot. Fire up the deck barbecue as the cliff turns gold, and keep an eye out, since the bay is open enough that a night northerly can find its way in.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the submerged ruins along the cliff
Hike up to the Lydae ruins
Barbecue on deck at sunset
Swim off the bow in the warm shallows
4.mooringTip: Ruin Bay has free taverna buoys and good sand at five to seven metres, but it opens to the north, so check the forecast before committing to the night. If a northerly is due, Kapı Creek or Tersane Island a short hop away are calmer bets.

5.shortDescription: Six miles to Göcek marina, with a swim stop at the low Yassıca Islands or Domuz Adası before tying up for showers and shops.
5.description: Halfway through the week it is worth trading a wild bay for a proper marina, and Göcek is the obvious call. It is only six miles, so make a morning of the islands on the way. The Yassıca group sits barely above the water, a cluster of flat rock and turquoise channels where the fish hang thick over the seagrass; drop the hook, swim, and let lunch run long. Domuz Adası nearby offers the same clear shallows if Yassıca is busy. Come into D-Marin in the early afternoon, hit the fuel berth by the entrance first, then take a lazy line and back onto the pontoon. The evening is for solid ground: a shower, an ice cream, a wander round the chandlers and pottery shops, and a table at one of the meyhanes off the main street.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the seagrass channels at Yassıca Islands
Swim off Domuz Adası's clear shallows
Browse the pottery shops at D-Marin Göcek
Dinner at a Göcek meyhane off the square
5.mooringTip: D-Marin Göcek is stern-to with lazy lines and runs roughly a hundred to a hundred and sixty euros a night in peak season, so book ahead in July and August. Refuel at the fuel berth by the entrance before you take your berth, not after.

6.shortDescription: A short five miles to Sarsala, a pine-walled inlet with no marina and no lights, saved for a last night of stars and barbecue.
6.description: Stock the fridge and top up water in Göcek before you leave, because Sarsala has nothing but pines and quiet. It is a five-mile amble down the gulf to a deep, green inlet ringed by hillside forest and sheltered from more or less any direction the wind decides to blow. Anchor on sand in four to six metres and take a long line back to a tree if you want to lie still overnight. The shallows at the head are worth a slow paddle; herons work the margins at dawn and dusk, and there is usually an octopus or two along the rocky edges for anyone with a mask. Save your best provisions for tonight. Grill something simple on deck, kill the cabin lights, and let the sky do the rest away from any town glow.
thingsToDo: Anchor with a long line to the pines
Snorkel the rocky edges for octopus
Watch herons in the shallows at dusk
Stargaze with no town light nearby
6.mooringTip: Sarsala is free anchoring on good sand at four to six metres, usually with a long line ashore to a tree because the inlet is narrow. There are no shops or water here, so provision fully in Göcek before you come down.

7.shortDescription: The last twelve miles to Fethiye, timed for an early-afternoon arrival, with a final swim below the Lycian rock tombs and a market wander.
7.description: Home day, and no reason to make it a chore. It is twelve miles back to the marina, so slip out mid-morning while the water is still oily and the breeze has not built. Point east-southeast and let it be a sail if the wind cooperates. There is time for one more swim on the way, ideally under the rock-cut Lycian tombs carved high into the cliffs behind Fethiye, which look their best from the water. If it is a Tuesday, the town market is in full swing, so tie up early and go for figs, olives and a bag of pomegranates. Aim to be alongside at ECE by around two, which leaves the afternoon to refuel, rinse the salt off and pack. Confirm your handover slot the day before so the checkout runs clean.
thingsToDo: Final swim beneath the Lycian rock tombs
Shop the Tuesday market in Fethiye town
Refuel and rinse down at ECE Marina
Pomegranate juice at a quayside café
7.mooringTip: Marina di Fethiye ECE is stern-to with lazy lines, roughly seventy to a hundred and ten euros a night in peak season. Refuel at the entrance fuel berth on your way in, and confirm your handover time twenty-four hours ahead so the berth and checkout are ready.

gocek-7-days.metaDesc: A week-long sailing loop from Göcek through Sarsala, Fethiye, Gemiler, Ölüdeniz, Kapı Creek and Ruin Bay across the Gulf of Fethiye.

1.shortDescription: An easy five-mile warm-up south from D-Marin Göcek into Sarsala, a pine-ringed cove that stays calm whatever the breeze does.
1.description: First day out is rarely about covering ground. We usually cast off from D-Marin Göcek around midday, once the provisioning is stowed and the crew has stopped rummaging for their swimsuits, and point south for Sarsala. Five miles later you drop the hook on sand in four to six metres, tucked under pine ridges that seem to close in from every side. The wind here is the imbat, a thermal sea breeze that fills in from the west by early afternoon at ten to fifteen knots and fades once the sun drops. Nothing like the Aegean Meltemi. Swim off the transom, then walk the ridge path through thyme and pine for a look back over the bay. Nights are dark and quiet. Grill something on the stern, count satellites, and turn in early.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the pine-cliff shoreline for bream
Walk the thyme-and-pine ridge above the bay
Grill fresh fish on the stern at dusk
Stargaze with zero light pollution
1.mooringTip: Sarsala is free anchoring on good sand at four to six metres, sheltered from every direction, so there is no need to book. There is no marina or reliable shop, so top up water and provisions in Göcek before you leave.

2.shortDescription: Twelve miles east to Fethiye, where Lycian tombs are cut into the cliff above town and the Tuesday market fills the back streets.
2.description: Fethiye is the one proper town on this loop, so it earns a night. The passage runs about twelve miles east-southeast, and if the imbat cooperates you can sail most of it on a beam reach. Tie up at Marina di Fethiye, the ECE marina, and the town is a short walk along the front. Above the houses, the rock-cut tomb of Amyntas stares down from the cliff, a full Ionic façade carved into the stone in the fourth century BC. The climb up is worth doing before the heat builds. Wander the Paspatur bazaar afterwards, then head for the fish market, where you buy your catch from the stalls and a surrounding restaurant grills it for you. If you happen to be here on a Tuesday, the sprawling weekly market is reason enough to stay ashore.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Amyntas rock-cut tomb
Buy and grill your catch at the fish market
Wander the Paspatur old bazaar
Hit the Tuesday street market for spices
2.mooringTip: Marina di Fethiye (ECE) is stern-to with lazy lines, roughly €70–110 a night in peak season and fully sheltered. Book a day or two ahead in July and August, and refuel at the fuel berth by the entrance on the way in.

3.shortDescription: Eight miles south to Gemiler Island, the Byzantine St Nicholas site, then anchor opposite in Coldwater Bay where cold springs chill the sea.
3.description: Gemiler Island, marked on some charts as St Nicholas Island, is one of the quieter surprises of the gulf. Eight miles south of Fethiye, its slopes are covered in Byzantine ruins, churches and a covered processional way that climbs to the summit, built between the fourth and sixth centuries when this was a pilgrimage stop tied to St Nicholas of Myra, long before his remains were carried off to Bari in 1087. Go ashore in the late afternoon, once the tour boats thin out. For the night, drop back across to Coldwater Bay, Soğuksu on the mainland side. Freshwater springs bleed straight into the sea here, and you feel it the moment you swim through a cold patch on an otherwise warm surface. A local trick worth trying at least once.
thingsToDo: Walk the Byzantine processional path on Gemiler
Swim through the cold freshwater springs
Catch sunset over the Gemiler ruins
Order meze at a Coldwater Bay jetty
3.mooringTip: Coldwater Bay works on the restaurant-buoy system: pick up a free mooring and settle the deal by eating ashore. If you would rather anchor, there is sand at five to seven metres, sheltered from the north.

4.shortDescription: A short four-mile hop to Butterfly Valley's cliff-walled canyon, then round to Ölüdeniz for the night under paragliders drifting off Babadağ.
4.description: Only four miles today, but they lead somewhere memorable. Butterfly Valley sits at the foot of a canyon that rises 350 metres of near-vertical rock, and in high summer it fills with Jersey tiger butterflies, orange-and-cream wings you will spot on the walk up to the waterfall at the back of the gorge. Anchor off the pebble beach, go ashore, and be back aboard before the afternoon wind funnels down the valley. This is a daytime stop, not a berth for the night. Move the last mile or so round to Ölüdeniz, where the famous lagoon curls behind its sandspit and paragliders spiral down off Babadağ, the mountain standing 1,969 metres over the beach. Watching them land while you swim off the boat never quite gets old.
thingsToDo: Hike the gorge to Butterfly Valley waterfall
Spot the endemic Jersey tiger butterflies
Swim off the boat at Ölüdeniz
Watch paragliders land from Babadağ
4.mooringTip: Butterfly Valley is a day-anchorage only, on rock at six to eight metres, and a gusty katabatic wind runs down the canyon by afternoon, so do not linger past mid-afternoon. Overnight instead at Ölüdeniz on sand in five to seven metres.

5.shortDescription: A twelve-mile run back into Skopea Limanı to Kapı Creek, a fjord-like slot barely eighty metres wide at its mouth.
5.description: After the open feel of Ölüdeniz, Kapı Creek comes as a complete contrast. The inlet cuts deep into the hills of Skopea Limanı and narrows to about eighty metres at the entrance, so you nose in through a gap that genuinely feels like a fjord before the water opens out inside. It is protected from anything the weather throws at it. Grab a buoy off the little restaurant at the head, where a pebble beach makes an easy swim step. Late in the afternoon, paddle the tender out to the narrows and back for the light on the cliffs. Dinner ashore usually runs to grilled fish, mountain herbs and a glass of rakı, and if there is local thyme honey on the table, buy a jar. With the cliffs closing overhead, the stars come out framed in a narrow strip.
thingsToDo: Swim off the pebble beach at the creek head
Paddle the tender to the eighty-metre narrows
Try rakı and thyme honey ashore
Stargaze framed by the creek walls
5.mooringTip: Kapı Creek runs on restaurant buoys: the mooring is free if you eat at the taverna at the head of the inlet. Space is tight and popular in season, so arrive by mid-afternoon to be sure of a buoy, or anchor on sand at five to seven metres.

6.shortDescription: Four gentle miles to Ruin Bay, where a drowned Lycian settlement lies just beneath the surface and Roman columns rest at snorkel depth.
6.description: Another easy four miles brings you to Ruin Bay, sometimes called Tomb Bay, and the name is earned. Under the cliffs at the water's edge lie the remains of a Lycian settlement that slipped below the sea centuries ago. Mask and fins are all you need: Roman column drums and worked stone sit in two or three metres of clear water, close enough to reach on a single breath. Above the bay, the ruins of ancient Lydae stand half-swallowed by olive trees and grazing goats, a rough scramble uphill for anyone who wants the walk. We tend to leave the ruins for late afternoon and spend the middle of the day in the water. A barbecue on the stern, köfte and cold beer, closes it out well. Warm nights, still water, no reason to rush.
thingsToDo: Snorkel over the submerged Roman columns
Scramble up to the Lydae ruins
Grill lamb köfte on the stern at sunset
Dive off the bow into warm water
6.mooringTip: Ruin Bay uses the same restaurant-buoy setup, free with dinner ashore and sheltered from the north. If the buoys are taken, anchor on sand at five to seven metres and take the tender in.

7.shortDescription: Six easy miles back to Göcek, with a last swim among the low, sandy Yassıca Islands before you refuel and hand the boat back.
7.description: The last leg is short, only six miles back to D-Marin Göcek, so there is time for one more swim. The Yassıca Islands lie roughly on the way, a scatter of low, rocky islets with sandy spits and shallow water that turns pale green over the bottom. Drop the hook, jump in, and let the crew have a proper last morning before the boat has to be tidied. Aim to be alongside in Göcek by around two, which leaves the afternoon to refuel, rinse the salt off and sort the gear for handover. Göcek itself rewards a wander: the marina promenade is lined with small boutiques and cafés, and more than one shop will let you taste local saffron-scented honey before you buy. A quiet dinner in town is a fitting way to end the week.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at the Yassıca Islands
Browse the D-Marin promenade boutiques
Taste local saffron honey in town
Refuel and rinse down before handover
7.mooringTip: D-Marin Göcek is stern-to on lazy lines, around €100–160 a night at peak and fully sheltered. Confirm your handover slot 24 hours out, and top up at the fuel berth by the entrance before you take the berth.

gocek-kizikuyruk-koyu.metaDesc: A 7-day sailing loop from Göcek out to Ekincik and the Dalyan delta, then back via Gemiler Island, Fethiye and Wall Bay through the Twelve Islands.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 8-mile warm-up south from Göcek, swimming off the Yassıca Islands before tucking into the narrow, cliff-walled Kapı Creek for dinner.
1.description:
The first leg is deliberately easy. Eight miles down from D-Marin Göcek gives the crew time to find the winches and the kettle before anything demanding. We usually break the run at the Yassıca Islands, a low scatter of rock and turquoise shallows that begs for a first swim off the transom.

Kapı Creek sits at the far end of Skopea Limanı, the tight cluster of coves and islets that makes this the densest cruising ground on the coast. The creek itself is a slot between pine-topped cliffs, barely wider than a couple of boat lengths. Pick up a restaurant buoy, and dinner buys you the mooring. The water is glassy by evening, the cliffs throw back the last of the light, and şakşuka eaten on deck tastes better than it has any right to.
thingsToDo: Swim the turquoise shallows off Yassıca Islands
Snorkel the pine-shadowed cliff base of Kapı Creek
Dinner at a Kapı Creek restaurant buoy
Stargaze framed between the creek walls
1.mooringTip: Grab one of the restaurant buoys deep in the creek; the mooring is free once you commit to dinner ashore. If they are all taken, drop the hook on sand in 5 to 7 metres. The cove is fully protected whatever the forecast.

2.shortDescription: A 20-mile reach west to Ekincik, the jumping-off bay for a river trip up to the Kaunos rock tombs and İztuzu's turtle beach.
2.description:
Twenty miles is the longest hop of the week, so we like to slip the buoy early and make the most of any morning breeze before it fills in from the west. Ekincik opens up as a broad, pine-backed bay with good holding on sand, well tucked from the northerlies.

The bay is really a base camp. You leave the yacht here and take a local river boat into the Dalyan delta, where the current threads between towering reed beds up to Köyceğiz Lake. The Lycian tombs of Kaunos are cut straight into the cliff above the water, four centuries before Christ and still staring down at every passing boat. İztuzu beach at the river mouth is a protected loggerhead nesting strip, so the sand stays refreshingly undeveloped. Those who want the full ritual finish at the Sultaniye mud baths.
thingsToDo: River-boat trip up the Dalyan delta
See the Kaunos Lycian rock tombs
Walk İztuzu's protected turtle beach
Soak in the Sultaniye mud baths
2.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in the middle of Ekincik Bay in 5 to 7 metres; holding is reliable and the northerlies pass right over. If a swell works in from the south, the restaurant pontoons on the southern shore are the calmer bet.

3.shortDescription: A 20-mile run back east to Gemiler Island, ruined Byzantine chapels above the water and a startling cold-spring swim in the bay opposite.
3.description:
Another long-ish leg, this time east-southeast, and often the best sailing of the trip if the afternoon breeze cooperates. Gemiler Island rises off the port bow, its whole flank terraced with the ruins of Byzantine churches and a covered processional walkway that once sheltered pilgrims from sun and rain.

This is the island long tied to Saint Nicholas of Myra, whose bones were carried off to Bari in the eleventh century. Ashore, the paths climb past cisterns and half-standing apses with the sea right below you. We anchor across the channel in Coldwater Bay, where springs push up through the seabed and the top layer of water turns suddenly, bracingly cold. Swim down a metre and you feel the line. Dinner ashore, a plate of meze glazed with pomegranate, and the ruins go gold at dusk.
thingsToDo: Climb the Byzantine ruins of Gemiler Island
Find the cold-spring layer in Coldwater Bay
Walk the covered pilgrim processional way
Pomegranate-glazed meze at a bay taverna
3.mooringTip: Coldwater Bay across the channel has restaurant buoys thrown out for the season; take one and eat ashore, or anchor on sand in 5 to 7 metres nearby. Gemiler's own anchorage can get choppy in an afternoon westerly, so the bay opposite is usually the calmer overnight.

4.shortDescription: A short six-mile hop north into a quiet pebble cove ringed with pine, with sea caves to paddle and a ridge walk above the anchorage.
4.description:
After two big passages, day four is a reward: barely six miles up to Kızıkuyruk Koyu, a pebble-lined cove folded into the pines. Drop the hook on sand in four to six metres and you have most of the day to do very little.

The south wall of the cove hides a run of low sea caves, shallow enough to poke into by kayak or paddleboard when the water is calm. Snorkellers will find sea urchins wedged in the rocks and the odd starfish on the sandy patches between. For anyone with restless legs, a rough track climbs the thyme-scented ridge behind the beach to a broad view back over the water. Come evening we fire up the grill: prawns, lemon, a scatter of oregano, eaten with feet still damp. Quiet nights here, once the day boats leave.
thingsToDo: Paddle the sea caves on the south wall
Snorkel the rocks for urchins and starfish
Hike the thyme ridge for a sea view
Grill lemon-oregano prawns on deck
4.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in 4 to 6 metres tucked under the northern shore, which keeps the meltemi off your back. It is a small cove, so arrive by mid-afternoon in July and August or take one of the restaurant lines on the south side instead.

5.shortDescription: Six miles east into Fethiye for a marina berth, a bustling town, and the Ionic façade of the Amyntas tomb carved high in the cliff.
5.description:
A gentle six miles brings you into Fethiye, and after several nights at anchor a proper berth has its appeal: shore power, a hot shower, and a town that actually stays up late. We tie up stern-to at the marina, lazy lines handed across by the berthing crew.

Fethiye earns a full afternoon. The headline sight is the tomb of Amyntas, a full Ionic temple front cut into the rock face above the rooftops, best climbed in the cooler evening light. Down in the old quarter, the lanes open onto the fish market, where you buy your catch and a nearby cook grills it for a small fee. If you land on a Tuesday, the sprawling market is worth an hour for spices and hand-knotted rugs. Order kabak çiçeği dolması, toast the week with cold rakı, and wander the waterfront.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Amyntas rock tomb at dusk
Pick your fish at Fethiye fish market
Browse the Tuesday market for rugs and spices
Kabak çiçeği dolması at a back-street lokanta
Cold rakı on the harbour front
5.mooringTip: Berth stern-to on lazy lines at the town marina; the crew take your lines and the basin is fully enclosed against any wind. Peak-season nights run roughly €70 to €110 depending on boat length, and it pays to radio ahead in high summer.

6.shortDescription: A short six miles south to Wall Bay, a steep-sided limestone cove with no marina, good holding and the week's best snorkelling.
6.description:
The shortest leg of the week, and by now the crew can almost do it in their sleep. Six miles south of Fethiye the coast folds in on itself and Wall Bay appears, hemmed by pale limestone that drops straight into deep, clear water.

There is no marina and no village here, which is rather the point. Provision in Fethiye before you leave, because the only thing on offer in the bay is the swimming, and that is excellent. Mask on, follow the cliff base and you find underwater clefts and gorges where octopus tuck into the cracks and parrotfish drift across the rock. Paddle round to a smaller beach for a lazy lunch of olive bread and labneh. The holding is good on sand in four to six metres, and once the sun drops behind the wall the stars come out hard.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the underwater gorges for octopus
Spot parrotfish along the limestone drop-off
Paddle to the far beach for lunch
Olive bread and labneh on the foredeck
Watch the stars over the cliff wall
6.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in 4 to 6 metres well in under the cliffs; the wall itself blocks the northerly. There is no marina, water or shop in the bay, so top up fuel, water and food in Fethiye before you cast off.

7.shortDescription: A final eight miles back to Göcek, with a swim over Tersane Island's sunken shipyard before you refuel and hand the boat back at D-Marin.
7.description:
The last morning is short and unhurried, eight miles back up to Göcek. There is no reason to rush the handover, so we leave time for one final swim on the way. Tersane Island is the spot: anchor in its almost landlocked cove and snorkel over the remains of an old shipyard, low walls and slipways still visible through the shallows.

From there it is a straight run to D-Marin Göcek. Call in at the fuel berth by the entrance before you tie up, so the boat goes back topped off. Berthing is stern-to on lazy lines and the marina is fully protected, though a base this smart charges for it, roughly €100 to €160 a night in peak weeks. Once the lines are on, walk the promenade, browse the boutiques, and mark the end of the week with a Turkish coffee under the bougainvillea.
thingsToDo: Snorkel Tersane Island's sunken shipyard
Top up at the D-Marin fuel berth
Walk the D-Marin promenade boutiques
Turkish coffee under the bougainvillea
7.mooringTip: Refuel at the D-Marin fuel berth just inside the entrance before you take your berth, so you hand the boat back full. Mooring is stern-to on lazy lines in a fully sheltered basin; expect roughly €100 to €160 a night in high season and book your return slot ahead.

gokova-gulf-14-days.metaDesc: A two-week loop from Bodrum through the Gökova gulf, then north to Güllük and Didim. Mud baths at Kara Ada, Cleopatra's golden sand, quiet pine coves.

1.shortDescription: A gentle first hop out of the marina to Kara Ada, where a cave spring and orange mud make an easy shakedown afternoon.
1.description: You won't need an early start for this one. Kara Ada sits barely five miles off Bodrum, a green, pine-topped hump that most crews reach inside an hour under engine. The draw is the cave on the eastern shore, where a sulphur spring runs at body temperature and the mud around it stains an odd orange. People smear it on, bake in the sun, then rinse off in the bay. It's touristy, and by midday the gulet fleet has arrived, so I like to anchor early, do the mud circus before the crowds, then move to a quieter cove on the south side for lunch and a swim. Use the afternoon to sort the boat, check the tender and get provisions squared away. Tomorrow is a proper crossing.
thingsToDo: Soak in Kara Ada's sulphur cave spring
Coat yourself in the orange healing mud
Swim off the quieter south-side cove
Top up provisions before leaving Bodrum
1.mooringTip: Anchor off the cave in 4–8 m over sand and weed; it gets busy with day boats by late morning, so drop the hook early and run a line ashore if swing room is tight.

2.shortDescription: The big crossing south to Çökertme, a gusty cove of wooden jetties where taverna pontoons trade a mooring for dinner.
2.description: This is the day the trip opens up. Point the bow south across open water and you're looking at fifteen to twenty miles, three to four hours depending on the meltemi. Çökertme is the reward, a horseshoe cove backed by olive groves and a handful of family tavernas built out over the water. It's a classic gulet stop for good reason. The catch is the wind: gusts funnel down off the hills without warning, and the sand-and-weed bottom holds poorly. Most crews skip anchoring altogether and tie to one of the restaurant pontoons, which comes with a laid mooring line and an unspoken agreement to eat ashore. Captain İbrahim's has been feeding sailors here for years. Order the meze, watch the light go pink on the ridge, and turn in early.
thingsToDo: Dine on the pontoon at Captain İbrahim's
Order a full spread of Aegean meze
Watch sunset colour the ridge behind the cove
Swim before the afternoon gusts build
2.mooringTip: Forget the anchor here unless you enjoy re-setting it. Grab a taverna pontoon with a laid line instead; arrive by mid-afternoon in July as the three jetties fill fast, and expect sudden katabatic gusts overnight.

3.shortDescription: A short run east to Ören, a low-key village on the Keramos plain with a marina, a pebble beach and Carian ruins uphill.
3.description: After the drama of Çökertme, Ören feels calm. It's a modest coastal village on the south side of the gulf, set on the plain where the ancient city of Keramos once stood. The run east is easy, a couple of hours in flat morning water before the wind fills in. There's a small municipal marina and a long grey-pebble beach that stays quiet even in August, which is precisely the point. Walk into the old quarter and you'll find stone houses, bougainvillea and the scattered remains of Keramos on the slopes above town. It's not a place with much to prove. We usually take a slow lunch, hire bikes or walk up to the ruins in the cooler evening, and let the crew catch their breath before the head of the gulf tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Walk up to the ruins of ancient Keramos
Stroll Ören's stone-house old quarter
Unwind on the long grey-pebble beach
Tie up at the small municipal marina
3.mooringTip: Ören's little marina takes yachts on lazy lines and is rarely full; call ahead in peak weeks. If you prefer to anchor, the bay off the beach holds well in sand but is open to the afternoon westerly, so tuck as far in as depth allows.

4.shortDescription: Sail to the head of the gulf and Akyaka, where a spring-fed river winds through reed beds and thermal winds draw the kitesurfers.
4.description: Akyaka sits right at the top of the gulf, where the mountains close in and the water turns glassy green. The sail up is often a beat into the afternoon thermal, so we tend to leave Ören by nine and motor-sail the calmer early hours. The village itself is a delight of timber balconies and Ottoman-Greek architecture, all built under strict local rules that have kept the chain hotels out. Its signature is the Azmak, a short karst-fed river so clear you can count the fish; take the tender or a rented kayak up through the reeds to the springs and the riverside cafés. Out on the bay, the same steady wind that makes the climb hard turns Akyaka into one of Turkey's better kitesurfing spots. Eat grilled fish by the water and let the day slow down.
thingsToDo: Kayak the clear Azmak river to its springs
Lunch on grilled fish by the waterfront
Watch kitesurfers work the afternoon thermal
Wander Akyaka's timber-balconied old streets
4.mooringTip: The bay at the head of the gulf is deep and the afternoon wind sends in chop, so anchor close under the northern shore and take a line ashore. A few pontoon berths off the village restaurants let you dine and stay put if you'd rather.

5.shortDescription: Head back west to Sedir Island, home to Cleopatra's famous golden sand and the tumbled ruins of ancient Kedreai.
5.description: Sedir Island, better known as Cleopatra Island, is the one everyone wants to see, and for once the hype holds up. The beach is made of tiny golden oolite grains found almost nowhere else nearby, which is where the legend of Cleopatra shipping sand from Egypt comes from. Today the sand is fiercely protected: you walk a boardwalk, swim off the beach, but you cannot lie on it or carry a single grain away. Behind the shore lie the walls, theatre and agora of ancient Kedreai, worth the short wander in the morning cool. Come early. Day-tripper boats from Marmaris and Bodrum pour in from mid-morning, so we like to be anchored and ashore by eight, then gone by lunch to a quieter overnight cove nearby. The water here is a startling pale turquoise over sand.
thingsToDo: Walk the boardwalk along Cleopatra's golden beach
Explore the ruins and theatre of Kedreai
Snorkel the pale turquoise shallows off Sedir
Arrive early to beat the day-tripper fleet
5.mooringTip: There's no overnighting alongside the beach; anchor off the north or east of the island in sand, or better, visit at anchor by day and move to a sheltered cove for the night. Holding is good but the spot catches wash from constant tour-boat traffic.

6.shortDescription: A relaxed leg to Karacasöğüt, a deep pine-ringed bay of taverna jetties that shelters well when the meltemi pipes up.
6.description: Karaca, or Karacasöğüt, is one of the gulf's great all-weather holes. It sits on the southern shore, a deep inlet below Karaca Ada, wrapped in pine forest and so well protected that local boats winter here. After the exposure of Sedir, the calm is welcome. The bay splits into several arms, each with its own scatter of jetties and simple family restaurants where you tie up bows-to and eat under the trees. The sail across is gentle, maybe two to three hours, with the ridge feeding you a steady breeze on the beam. This is a day to do very little. Swim, read, dinghy into the reed-lined creek at the head of the bay, and settle in for a still night. If the forecast shows a big meltemi, there's nowhere better in the gulf to sit it out.
thingsToDo: Tie bows-to at a pine-shaded taverna jetty
Dinghy into the reed-lined creek at the head
Swim in the sheltered inner arms
Ride out a meltemi in calm water
6.mooringTip: Pick a restaurant pontoon and tie stern- or bows-to for a laid line and a guaranteed calm night; the water is deep close in, so approach slowly. Free-anchoring works in the arms too, in 6–10 m over mud that holds well.

7.shortDescription: Sail west toward the gulf mouth to Mersincik, a wild, empty anchorage inside the Gökova protected zone.
7.description: Mersincik is where the gulf starts to feel remote. Tucked on the north shore near the western mouth, it's part of the Gökova special protection zone, so there's no development, no road in, just pine, rock and clear water. The passage west takes you along the exposed northern side, and with a fresh meltemi you can carry good speed on a reach for much of it. The main bay is beautiful but catches the north wind; if it's blowing hard, duck into the smaller cove to the northwest, though swing room is tight and you'll want an anchor ashore. Wild goats pick along the shoreline in the evening. There's nothing to buy and nothing to do but swim, walk the empty headlands, and enjoy a night without a single light on shore. Fill water and provisions before you leave Karaca.
thingsToDo: Swim off the empty, undeveloped shoreline
Walk the pine headlands for gulf views
Spot wild goats along the shore at dusk
Stargaze with no shore lights around
7.mooringTip: Anchor in the main bay in 5–8 m over sand and take a line to the rocks; it's open to the north, so if the meltemi is up, use the tighter northwest cove and set a shore anchor as there's little room to swing. Arrive self-sufficient, as there are no facilities.

8.shortDescription: Cross back south to Çökertme for a second night on the taverna pontoons before the run home begins.
8.description: The loop turns for home today. From Mersincik you cross the mouth of the gulf back to the familiar horseshoe of Çökertme, a passage of a few hours that can be a fast, rolling reach if the meltemi cooperates. Second time around, you already know the cove's tricks. The same gusts pour off the hills, the same jetties wait, but you know the drill now. Slot onto a different pontoon than last week and try a new taverna; Rosemary and the other family kitchens along the shore each have their regulars. This is a good evening to sort laundry, refuel the crew on a long dinner ashore, and swap notes on the best swims of the trip so far. Watch the anchor light of late arrivals bob in as the wind gusts through after dark.
thingsToDo: Try a different taverna such as Rosemary
Swim the horseshoe cove one more time
Refuel the crew on a long shore dinner
Compare the trip's best swims over meze
8.mooringTip: As before, take a pontoon line rather than anchoring, and pick a different jetty from your first visit to keep things fresh. Rig a good snubber and extra fenders; the overnight gusts here nudge boats around on the laid moorings.

9.shortDescription: Retrace the crossing north-west to Kara Ada for a last gulf swim and a second go at the mud cave.
9.description: Back across the water to Kara Ada, closing the Gökova part of the trip. It's the same long open crossing as day two, run in reverse, so watch the forecast and leave early if the meltemi is set to build. By now the crew are proper sailors and the miles pass easily. Kara Ada makes a fitting last stop in the gulf, close enough to Bodrum that you're within reach of the marina but still a green island with that odd, restorative cave. Give the mud baths a second try if the first was too crowded, or just find a clean cove on the south side and swim. Rinse the salt, tidy the boat, and enjoy a quiet anchorage before the trip changes character. Tomorrow you round the peninsula and the whole feel of the coast shifts.
thingsToDo: Return to the mud cave for a quieter soak
Find a clean south-side cove to swim
Rinse and tidy the boat after the gulf
Toast the end of the Gökova loop
9.mooringTip: Same as day one: anchor over sand and weed near the cave, or move round to the south coves for cleaner holding and fewer boats by evening. Being so close to Bodrum, it fills with returning charter boats, so claim your spot before sundown.

10.shortDescription: Round the peninsula to fashionable Göltürkbükü, where beach clubs and jetty restaurants line a bay sheltered from the open-water meltemi.
10.description: Today the coast changes entirely. You round the western tip of the Bodrum peninsula, past Gümüşlük and Yalıkavak, onto the glamorous north shore and into Göltürkbükü. The channel outside can see the meltemi at a solid thirty knots, so time the rounding for the morning and reef early if in doubt. Inside, it's another world: a long, sheltered bay lined with wooden platforms built out over the sea, chic beach clubs and the famous Maçakızı hotel where Istanbul's smart set summers. This is the trip's dose of polish. Book a lounger, have a cocktail sent out to the boat, or simply people-watch from the cockpit as tenders ferry guests between yachts. It's a long way, in feel, from the empty headlands of Mersincik, and that contrast is part of the fun.
thingsToDo: Grab a lounger at a Türkbükü beach club
Dine on a jetty platform over the water
People-watch the tender traffic from the cockpit
See the Maçakızı hotel scene up close
10.mooringTip: Most of Göltürkbükü's waterfront is run by beach clubs that lay moorings and expect you to book a table or loungers; call ahead in season and they'll send a tender to take your lines. Free-anchoring room is limited and holding patchy, so a laid mooring is the easier call.

11.shortDescription: Sail north into the Gulf of Güllük to an old fishing town of seafood grills, a friendly marina and nearby Iasos ruins.
11.description: Güllük is the antidote to Türkbükü. A working fishing town on its own gulf north of the peninsula, it trades the celebrity gloss for trawlers, tea houses and some of the best-value seafood on this coast. The sail up is straightforward, out of Türkbükü and north into the wide, sheltered Gulf of Güllük, often a comfortable reach. There's a proper marina here with a few hundred berths if you want power and water, but the town quay is where the character sits. Wander the front in the evening, pick your fish off the ice at a lokanta, and eat it grilled with rakı and a gulf sunset. History buffs can take a taxi or a long walk to the ruins of ancient Iasos on the next headland, a Carian city that grew rich on exactly the fish you're eating. Quiet, honest, and a good re-provisioning stop.
thingsToDo: Pick fresh fish off the ice at a lokanta
Visit the Carian ruins of ancient Iasos
Stroll the working fishing-town waterfront
Re-provision at the market before Didim
11.mooringTip: Take a berth at Güllük's marina for full services, or go stern-to on the town quay for the livelier scene and easy reach of the fish restaurants. The gulf is well sheltered, so a night at anchor off the town in 4–6 m over mud is comfortable in settled weather.

12.shortDescription: A longer leg north to Didim's big marina, gateway to the Temple of Apollo and the golden beach at Altınkum.
12.description: Didim marks the top of the loop and the most built-up stop of the fortnight, but it earns its place. The passage up from Güllük is the longest of these northern days, so plan for an early departure and keep an eye on the meltemi, which can pick up across the open approaches. D-Marin Didim is a large, well-run marina with everything you might need to set the boat right for the run home. The real reason to come is inland: the Temple of Apollo at Didyma, one of the ancient world's great oracle sanctuaries, its huge columns still standing after more than two thousand years. It's a short, cheap taxi from the marina and worth an unhurried morning. Afterwards, cool off on the long golden sweep of Altınkum beach or grab lunch in town before turning south again.
thingsToDo: Tour the oracle Temple of Apollo at Didyma
Swim the long golden sands of Altınkum
Refit and refuel at D-Marin Didim
Lunch in Didim town after the ruins
12.mooringTip: D-Marin Didim is a full-service marina; radio ahead on VHF for a berth assignment, especially in high summer when catamaran and superyacht traffic is heavy. It's the easiest place on the route to take on fuel, water and fresh provisions before the two-day run back to Bodrum.

13.shortDescription: Turn south again and run back down to Göltürkbükü for a final polished night before the last hop to Bodrum.
13.description: The bow points home. From Didim you retrace the water south, back around into the lee of the Bodrum peninsula and into Göltürkbükü for a second, more relaxed night. Knowing the bay now, you can skip the scramble for a mooring and settle straight in. This is the last real evening of the trip, so it's worth making it count. If the first Türkbükü night was for the scene, use this one for a good dinner and an early, calm swim off the boat. The northern approaches can be breezy in the afternoon, so we usually leave Didim mid-morning to arrive with the light still high and the wind not yet at its peak. Pack in a final lazy afternoon on a jetty platform, sort gear and settle the last of the boat admin. Bodrum is only a short hop away tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Book a return table at a Türkbükü jetty club
Take a calm early swim off the boat
Enjoy a final lazy afternoon on a platform
Settle boat admin before the last leg
13.mooringTip: As on day ten, arrange a beach-club mooring ahead of arrival; being a return visit, you'll know which platform suited you. Aim to be secured by mid-afternoon before the northerly funnels into the bay and the tender queue builds.

14.shortDescription: The final short hop back around to Bodrum, with time for one last swim before handing the boat back.
14.description: The last morning is an easy one. Bodrum lies just around the eastern shoulder of the peninsula, a couple of hours from Göltürkbükü, so there's no need to rush the lines. Most charter bases want the boat back by late morning or midday, so we tend to slip out early, take one final swim at anchor somewhere along the way, then make our approach to the marina under the shadow of the great Castle of St Peter. Coming into Bodrum by sea is the right way to arrive: the fortress, the white town climbing the hills, the forest of masts in the harbour. Have fenders and lines ready well before you enter, follow the marineros' directions to your berth, and let the fortnight sink in. Two weeks, two gulfs, a mud cave and an oracle. Not a bad loop.
thingsToDo: Take a last swim at anchor en route
Approach under Bodrum's Castle of St Peter
Photograph the harbour's forest of masts
Hand the boat back before midday
14.mooringTip: Confirm your return time and berth with the charter base the evening before, and have fenders and mooring lines rigged on both sides before you enter Bodrum's busy harbour. Follow the marina staff in for a stern-to berth; the approach can be crowded with day boats at midday.

lycian-coast-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks round-trip from Göcek: sail Skopea Limani's coves, Fethiye's harbour, Ölüdeniz, Gemiler ruins and Ekincik for the Dalyan river trip.

1.shortDescription: Ease out of Göcek's marina belt into Skopea Limani and drop the hook in wide, pine-fringed Sarsala for a gentle first night.
1.description: Göcek is where nearly every Lycian charter begins, and there's no rush on day one. Sort provisions at the market by the square, top up water and ice, then motor out past the wooded islands of Skopea Limani. Sarsala sits on the north side of Boz Burun, a broad bay backed by red pine and a scatter of smaller coves. Holding is good over sand and weed, and most crews run a line ashore near the head to sit steady. There's a small jetty restaurant if you'd rather not cook the first evening. We usually aim to arrive by mid-afternoon, swim off the stern while the light drops behind the ridge, and let everyone find their sea legs before the longer legs ahead. Mornings here are glassy.
thingsToDo: Provision at Göcek's morning market
Swim off Sarsala's sandy jetty
Walk the pine track behind the bay
Dinner at the bay's jetty taverna
1.mooringTip: Anchor in 6-8 m over sand and weed and take a long line ashore toward the head of the bay. The western coves fill first in July, so arrive by early afternoon for a spot.

2.shortDescription: A short hop deeper into the gulf to Kapı Creek, a narrow fjord-like inlet with a friendly pontoon taverna at its head.
2.description: The run to Kapı Creek is barely an hour, so there's time for a lazy morning swim first. The creek cuts a slot between steep, tree-covered slopes, and it feels more like a Norwegian fjord than the Turkish coast until you catch the woodsmoke from the taverna. Yachts moor stern-to the wooden pontoon, where the family running the place take your lines and later bring out mezze and grilled fish. Water stays deep close in, so carry your speed toward the jetty and drop the hook well out. Go ashore for the short scramble up to the little goat pens above the head of the creek. It's a favourite with the flotilla crowd, so worth calling ahead in high summer to be sure of a berth.
thingsToDo: Moor stern-to the Kapı Creek pontoon
Order mezze and grilled fish ashore
Scramble to the goat pens above
Snorkel the steep rocky sides
2.mooringTip: Depths stay deep until close to the pontoon, so approach slowly and drop in 10-12 m before backing to the jetty for lazy lines. Radio or call ahead in July and August, as the handful of berths book out.

3.shortDescription: Cross to Ruin Bay, where cut Roman stone sits just under the surface at the head of the bay and the swimming is about the clearest in the gulf.
3.description: Ruin Bay, also called Cleopatra's Bath, lies a couple of hours west across the mouth of the gulf. The draw is underwater: low walls and cut stone from a Roman-era settlement rest just beneath the surface at the head of the bay, clear enough to read from a mask. Anchor off and swim in over the ruins rather than taking the boat too far into the shallows. A rickety jetty restaurant serves cold beer and simple grills under the pines. The neighbouring Tomb Bay, a short dinghy ride round the point, has Lycian rock tombs carved high into the cliff, worth a look in the soft evening light. Afternoons can bring a swirling breeze off the hills, so set the anchor with care.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the submerged Roman walls
Dinghy to Tomb Bay's Lycian tombs
Cold beer at the jetty grill
Photograph the cliffs at dusk
