3.mooringTip: The head of the bay shoals over the ruins, so anchor in 5-8 m clear of the stonework and swim ashore rather than motoring in. Watch for gusts funnelling down the valley in the afternoon and dig the anchor in well.

4.shortDescription: Round to Wall Bay beneath towering cliffs, tie stern-to the boat-only restaurant, and dine with your feet almost over the water.
4.description: Wall Bay, Duvar Bükü on the chart, takes its name from the sheer rock face that walls in one side of the anchorage. It's a short sail from Ruin Bay, so plan a swim stop on the way. The bottom drops off quickly, which makes free swinging room tight, so most boats go stern-to the small pontoon belonging to Adaia, the family restaurant here you can only reach by boat. They'll help with your lines and cook a proper Turkish spread once the sun is off the cliffs. Snorkelling along the wall is among the best in this corner of the gulf, with clear water and steep drop-offs. Quiet after dark, with only cicadas and the odd goat bell for company.
thingsToDo: Tie stern-to Adaia's boat-only pontoon
Snorkel the sheer underwater wall
Try the restaurant's Turkish mezze spread
Kayak along the cliff base
4.mooringTip: The bottom shelves steeply, so free-anchoring space is limited; take a line to the restaurant pontoon and reserve a table, which usually secures your berth. Drop in 8-10 m and back in close under the rock.

5.shortDescription: Leave the coves for a night in Fethiye, a working harbour town with a lively fish market, bazaar, and rock tombs above.
5.description: Time to reprovision and stretch your legs in a real town. Fethiye sits at the head of its own gulf, a couple of hours' sail from Wall Bay, usually with a helpful afternoon breeze on the beam. Berth at Ece Marina or the town quay and walk into the bazaar for spices, Turkish delight, and a barber if you fancy one. The fish market is the ritual here: buy your catch from the stalls, then hand it to one of the surrounding restaurants who grill it for a small charge. Above the town, the rock-cut Tomb of Amyntas glows amber at sunset, a stiff climb up the steps but worth it. Stock the fridge properly before you head back out to the wilder bays.
thingsToDo: Buy fish at the covered market
Climb to the Tomb of Amyntas
Browse the old bazaar for spices
Reprovision at the harbourside shops
5.mooringTip: Ece Marina is calm and well run, but book ahead in peak weeks; the town quay is cheaper and central, though it can roll when ferries pass. Med-moor to laid lines and expect marina rates.

6.shortDescription: Sail out of Fethiye Gulf to Gemiler Island, ringed by Byzantine church ruins and one of the coast's finest sunset walks.
6.description: Gemiler, sometimes called St Nicholas Island, guards the entrance to a sheltered channel southwest of Fethiye. The whole island is layered with Byzantine ruins: churches, cisterns, and a covered processional way climbing to a chapel at the summit. Anchor in the cove on the island's south side in 6 to 9 metres over sand, and run a line to the shore to keep from swinging. Two simple restaurants ashore handle water and a cold drink. Leave time to land the dinghy and walk up through the ruins as the light goes gold; the view over the channel to the mainland is why people linger here. The anchorage is calm in the usual westerlies but exposed if the wind backs to the south.
thingsToDo: Walk the ruins to the summit chapel
Time the climb for golden hour
Line ashore in the south cove
Snorkel the channel to the shore
6.mooringTip: Anchor in 6-9 m on sand in the southern cove and take a line ashore to the rocks, as swinging room is limited. Arrive by late afternoon in season, since the handful of good spots go early.

7.shortDescription: A short leg to Ölüdeniz, home to the famous Blue Lagoon sand spit and the paragliders drifting down from Babadağ above.
7.description: Ölüdeniz is the postcard everyone knows, a still lagoon behind a curl of white sand under a green mountain. The lagoon itself is a protected nature park now and closed to yachts, so you moor under the headland off Belcekız beach and go ashore by dinghy or swim in. Overhead there's a near-constant stream of paragliders spiralling down from Babadağ, which at almost two thousand metres ranks among the highest commercial launch sites anywhere. This is the busiest spot on the route, so treat it as a lively day stop rather than a wilderness night. Watch a canopy or two land on the beach, eat an ice cream, then decide whether to stay put or slip back to a quieter cove.
thingsToDo: Swim into the Blue Lagoon nature park
Watch paragliders land on Belcekız beach
Dinghy ashore for beachfront lunch
Walk the sand spit at dusk
7.mooringTip: The lagoon is closed to boats, so anchor off Belcekız under the headland in 8-12 m and expect a rolly, exposed night if any southerly runs in. Many crews use it as a day stop and move on to a calmer anchorage.

8.shortDescription: Drop the hook off Butterfly Valley, a steep gorge that spills to a boat-only beach with a waterfall walk inland.
8.description: Just around the headland, Butterfly Valley opens as a cleft in the flank of Babadağ, its beach reachable only from the water. The valley takes its name from the tiger butterflies that breed here, and a rough path leads inland past a small farm to a waterfall at the back of the gorge. Wear proper shoes; the last stretch means scrambling over rock with a rope to help. The anchorage is a day stop for most, open to the south and deep close to shore, so you drop in fairly deep and run a long line to the beach. Have lunch aboard, do the hike in the cool of the morning, and move on before the afternoon breeze turns it uncomfortable. Ashore there's a rustic bar under the trees.
thingsToDo: Hike the gorge to the waterfall
Look for tiger butterflies inland
Anchor with a long line ashore
Drink at the rustic beach bar
8.mooringTip: This is an open day anchorage, deep right up to the beach; drop in 15-20 m and run a long line ashore, keeping an anchor watch. Leave before the afternoon southwesterly builds, as it sends swell straight in.

9.shortDescription: Head back into Skopea Limani to Coldwater Bay, where springs off the hillside chill the sea and the swimming is bracing.
9.description: With the exposed southern bays behind you, the route turns back into the shelter of Skopea Limani and Coldwater Bay, known to the charts as Soğuksu. Freshwater springs run off the wooded slope straight into the sea, and you feel the cold in patches as you swim, a jolt after the warm water elsewhere. It's a peaceful, pine-scented anchorage with good holding and room to take a line ashore among the trees. There's usually a small pontoon restaurant tucked into one corner if you want dinner brought to you. After the crush at Ölüdeniz, this feels like the coast exhaling. We often stay two nights when the schedule allows, using it as a base to swim, read, and let the day drift.
thingsToDo: Swim through the cold spring patches
Line ashore among the pines
Dinner at the corner pontoon restaurant
Paddleboard the calm morning water
9.mooringTip: Holding is good over sand and weed in 6-10 m; take a line ashore to the trees, since the bay is narrow with limited swinging room. It stays calm in the prevailing westerlies, making it a reliable overnight.

10.shortDescription: Return to Gemiler Island for a second night, taking the ruins at a slower pace and staking out a good sunset spot early.
10.description: Looping back to Gemiler on the way west gives you the island a second time without the pressure of ticking it off. Arrive earlier than the day-trip gulets and you'll have the pick of the south cove, with time to snorkel the channel before the afternoon crowd thins toward evening. If you skipped the full climb last time, do it now: the path threads through roofless churches and old cisterns up to the summit chapel, best walked as the heat eases. Back aboard, the anchorage settles once the tripper boats leave and the sunset over the ruins is the quiet reward. Grab water and a cold drink from the restaurants ashore. It's a short, easy day, so no need to leave Coldwater Bay early.
thingsToDo: Revisit the summit chapel path
Snorkel the island channel at midday
Claim a south-cove spot early
Sunset drinks as the gulets leave
10.mooringTip: As before, anchor in 6-9 m of sand in the southern cove with a line ashore; getting in before the afternoon gulet traffic secures a calmer, better-placed berth. Watch for a southerly shift that would make it untenable overnight.

11.shortDescription: The longest leg of the trip, west to Ekincik, launch point for the river run up to Kaunos and Dalyan's mud baths.
11.description: Ekincik is the western turn of the route and the longest single hop, so we usually leave by nine to be in before the afternoon breeze builds on the nose. The bay is broad and sandy, set below the hills at the edge of the Dalyan delta, with a small two-pontoon marina in one corner if you'd rather tie up than anchor. The real reason to come is what lies beyond: local boats run from here up the reed-lined Dalyan river to the rock-cut tombs of ancient Kaunos, the sulphur mud baths at Sultaniye, and İztuzu, the turtle-nesting beach at the river mouth. Arrange the trip through the marina or a beach restaurant the evening you arrive. The open bay can send in a swell if the wind goes south.
thingsToDo: Book the Dalyan river boat trip
Wallow in the Sultaniye mud baths
See the rock tombs of Kaunos
Walk İztuzu turtle beach at the delta
11.mooringTip: Anchor in 5-10 m over good sand in the northern corner, or take a berth at the small My Marina pontoons for more security. The bay is open to the south and southwest, so pick a settled forecast for an overnight here.

12.shortDescription: A gentle sail east to quiet Kızılkuyruk, a wooded cove off the main track where the night is all cicadas and stars.
12.description: After the buzz of the Dalyan trip, Kızılkuyruk is the antidote: a small, tree-lined bay on the Boz Burun peninsula that sees few boats and keeps things simple. The sail from Ekincik is short and easy, hugging a coast of empty coves and steep pine. There's nothing ashore to speak of, which is the point; you anchor, run a line to a tree, and swim in water that stays clear and still. Bring what you need for the evening, since there's no taverna to fall back on. This is the sort of anchorage crews remember long after the headline stops fade, a last taste of the quiet before the route turns for home. Stars come out thick once the light dies.
thingsToDo: Line ashore to a shady pine
Swim in the empty clear cove
Cook aboard under the stars
Kayak the deserted neighbouring coves
12.mooringTip: Anchor in 6-10 m and take a line to a tree, as the cove is narrow with little swinging room. It's well protected in the usual summer westerlies but has no facilities, so provision and fill water at Ekincik first.

13.shortDescription: Sail back into Skopea Limani to Sarsala, closing the loop where it began with an easy final night at anchor.
13.description: The penultimate day brings you back into the familiar shelter of Skopea Limani and round to Sarsala, the same broad bay that opened the trip. Coming back to it at the end feels different: you know the drill now, pick your line ashore without fuss, and settle in like regulars. The sail is a relaxed reach across the gulf with time for one last long swim stop en route. Sarsala's holding and its jetty restaurant make it an easy final night, close enough to Göcek that the morning run back stays stress-free. Use the afternoon to swim out the last of your energy, sort gear, and toast the fortnight. If the taverna's busy, book a table when you drop the hook.
thingsToDo: Last long swim stop mid-gulf
Final dinner at Sarsala's jetty taverna
Line ashore near the bay head
Sort and pack gear before Göcek
13.mooringTip: Return to the same routine: anchor in 6-8 m over sand and weed with a line ashore near the head of the bay. Being close to base, you can afford a leisurely start, but reserve the taverna early if you want to eat ashore.

14.shortDescription: A short morning motor back to Göcek to hand back the boat, with time for a last coffee on the waterfront.
14.description: The final leg is barely an hour, so there's no need for an early alarm. Most charters want the boat back mid-morning, which leaves time for a quiet swim before you weigh anchor and idle out of Sarsala for the last time. The run back across Skopea Limani retraces the very first day, the wooded islands sliding past in reverse. Top up fuel and water on the way in if the base asks for it, then bring her alongside in Göcek and let the crew handle the lines. Once you're checked out, there's usually time for a coffee or a last lunch on the waterfront before transfers. Fourteen days of it, and the gulf still holds coves you didn't reach.
thingsToDo: Last dawn swim off Sarsala
Top up fuel before handback
Coffee on the Göcek waterfront
Browse the marina shops before transfer
14.mooringTip: Confirm the base's fuel-up and return-time rules the night before so you're not rushed. Approach Göcek's marina slowly and call ahead on VHF for berth directions; staff take your lines on arrival.
